title: Liquidity Fades as Treasuries Age - Liberty Street Economics
gist: Using Treasury TRACE data, NY Fed researchers show that liquidity collapses almost immediately once a Treasury note or bond loses on-the-run (benchmark) status — volume and trade frequency drop by an order of magnitude and effective bid-ask spreads roughly double, except for off-the-run bonds that become cheapest-to-deliver into futures, which retain unusually strong trading activity.

themes.theme: Concentration of Treasury liquidity in benchmark issues

insights.claim: On-the-run Treasury securities — the most recently issued benchmarks, which make up less than 4 percent of the $30+ trillion outstanding Treasury debt — account for 65 percent of average daily trading volume.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Investors should treat the vast majority of the Treasury market (96% of outstanding face value) as structurally illiquid relative to the small benchmark slice; execution strategy and cost assumptions should differ sharply between on-the-run and off-the-run trades.
insights.quote: Less than 4 percent of this amount, which is associated with the most recently issued Treasuries, called on-the-run securities, accounts for 65 percent of average daily trading volume.
insights.locator: opening paragraph, More than $30 trillion U.S. Treasury debt

insights.claim: From January through June 2024, on-the-run notes and bonds averaged $262.9 billion per day in ATS/interdealer trading versus $174.8 billion in dealer-to-customer trading, while off-the-run notes and bonds averaged only $51.3 billion per day in ATS/interdealer trading versus $103.3 billion in dealer-to-customer trading.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: When a bond goes off-the-run, liquidity providers and trading venues change entirely, not just volume — off-the-run flow shifts to dealer intermediation, meaning investors need dealer relationships (not just electronic platforms) to trade seasoned issues efficiently.
insights.locator: table: On-the-Run Trading Is Concentrated in the Interdealer Market

insights.claim: Only 18 percent of customer trading activity in off-the-run Treasury securities has offsetting activity in the same security within the same fifteen-minute interval, implying dealers must warehouse inventory rather than simply matching buyers and sellers.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Thin two-sided flow in off-the-run bonds means dealers bear more inventory risk, which likely translates into wider spreads and reduced dealer risk appetite during stress — a mechanism investors should watch when sizing trades in seasoned issues.
insights.quote: only 18 percent of customer trading activity in off-the-run securities had offsetting activity in the same security within the same fifteen-minute interval
insights.locator: One reason for this change is that off-the-run securities are harder to match directly

themes.theme: Sharp seasoning cliff in volume and trade frequency

insights.claim: In the 2-year sector, the on-the-run note averages $56.3 billion in daily volume, dropping to $5.5 billion for the first off-the-run issue and $1.6 billion for the second off-the-run — roughly a 10x and 35x decline respectively.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: A single auction cycle (roughly one month for 2-years) triggers an order-of-magnitude collapse in tradability, so investors holding just-auctioned notes should expect a near-term liquidity cliff when the next auction reissues the benchmark.
insights.locator: Trading Volume Declines Once a Security Goes Off-the-Run

insights.claim: Trade frequency falls even more dramatically than volume: the on-the-run 5-year note trades about 37,900 times per day versus 465 times for the first off-the-run, and the on-the-run 10-year trades about 37,523 times per day versus 661 times for the first off-the-run.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: The ~50-80x drop in trade count versus a smaller drop in volume implies average trade sizes are rising even as the number of market participants willing to transact shrinks — a sign of a market moving from continuous price discovery to episodic block trading.
insights.locator: The decline in trading frequency is similarly large

insights.claim: Average trade size rises sharply as a bond goes off-the-run: in the 5-year sector from about $3 million (on-the-run) to $16 million (first off-the-run); in the 10-year sector from $2 million to $10 million; and in the 2-year sector from $4 million to $11 million.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Off-the-run trading is increasingly institutional/block-sized rather than retail or high-frequency — smaller investors may find it harder to get competitive execution in seasoned issues as fewer, larger trades dominate the tape.
insights.locator: Average Trade Size Initially Rises as Securities Move Off-the-Run

insights.claim: Off-the-run securities still account for roughly 30 percent of overall coupon trading volume, with the first and second off-the-runs contributing about 15 percent and deeper off-the-runs the remaining 15 percent.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Despite the liquidity cliff, off-the-run bonds are not a trivial sideshow — they remain a meaningful, tradable slice of the market, particularly the first two off-the-run issues, which retain outsized relevance versus deeper seasoned bonds.
insights.locator: The results also show that activity in the off-the-run segment is not uniform

insights.claim: Effective bid-ask spreads in the 2-year sector rise from 0.66 basis points for the on-the-run note to 1.22 basis points for the first off-the-run and 2.23 basis points for the second off-the-run — the move from on-the-run to first off-the-run roughly doubles transaction costs in several sectors.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Transaction cost models for Treasury portfolios should apply a roughly 2x haircut to liquidity assumptions the moment a bond loses benchmark status, materially affecting round-trip cost estimates for relative-value and basis trades.
insights.quote: In several sectors, the move from on-the-run to first off-the-run roughly doubles the effective bid-ask spread.
insights.locator: Lower Trading Activity Is Matched by Wider Effective Bid-Ask Spreads

insights.claim: In the March 2020 stress episode, the 2-year on-the-run effective spread widened only modestly (0.66 to 1.38 basis points) while the first off-the-run spread more than tripled (1.22 to 4.23 basis points) and the second off-the-run more than tripled again (2.23 to 7.45 basis points).
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Off-the-run Treasuries are disproportionately vulnerable in a liquidity crisis — investors and risk managers should assume seasoned-bond spreads can gap out 3x+ in a systemic stress event even as benchmark liquidity holds up comparatively well, a key input for tail-risk stress tests and margin/haircut policy.
insights.quote: These widenings suggest that the market's capacity to absorb selling pressure weakens more for seasoned securities at times of stress.
insights.locator: The liquidity pattern becomes even more pronounced in stressed conditions

insights.claim: For very deep off-the-run bonds, effective spread measures can understate true illiquidity in stress because the metric relies on executed trades — when a bond barely trades, the few observed trades may look artificially tight, masking the fact that infrequent trading is itself evidence of poor liquidity.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Anyone using transaction-based spread data to gauge liquidity risk in deep off-the-run bonds should treat unusually narrow readings with suspicion — near-zero trading volume, not a tight quoted spread, is the real liquidity red flag.
insights.quote: Infrequent trading is itself often a sign of poor liquidity.
insights.locator: There is one important caveat when interpreting the results

themes.theme: Cheapest-to-deliver status as a liquidity exception

insights.claim: Off-the-run bonds that become cheapest-to-deliver (CTD) into Treasury futures contracts break the general aging pattern, trading more actively than other off-the-run bonds of similar age because of their role in hedging, futures delivery, and basis trading.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Investors should not assume all off-the-run bonds are equally illiquid — identifying the current CTD issue for each futures contract flags a seasoned bond that will trade closer to on-the-run-like activity, useful for sourcing liquidity or for basis-trade construction.
insights.locator: Cheapest-to-Deliver Securities Are an Important Exception

insights.claim: Being the cheapest-to-deliver bond is associated with about $1.31 billion in additional daily trading volume in the 5-year sector, $0.75 billion in the 10-year sector, and $0.51 billion in the 2-year sector, with the 5-year CTD effect showing up broadly across dealer-to-customer, interdealer, and interdealer-broker activity.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: The CTD volume premium is largest in the 5-year sector, suggesting basis traders and hedgers concentrate the most futures-related activity there — a quantifiable liquidity bonus worth pricing into relative-value trades that reference CTD bonds.
insights.locator: we show that being the cheapest to deliver is associated with

insights.claim: The CTD exception demonstrates that off-the-run illiquidity is not purely a function of a bond's age; rather it reflects changes in investor use cases, the trading venues bonds rely on, and the degree of natural two-way flow — meaning structural demand (like futures delivery) can preserve liquidity despite seasoning.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: This reframes the liquidity-decay narrative: the driver isn't calendar age per se but loss of a functional role in the market, so any policy or product change that gives a security a persistent use case (e.g., new futures baskets, repo specialness) could keep it liquid despite being off-the-run.
insights.quote: They become less liquid because the uses investors have for them change, the trading venue they rely on changes, and/or the degree of natural two-way trading flow changes.
insights.locator: This exception matters because it shows that off-the-run securities
